Solution. We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
Answer. The reparametrization trick!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.

Setup. We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
Answer. Yes!!!
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation. Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!

I used LazyVim for a long time, but I kept hitting a wall: understanding how LazyVim did something was consistently harder than if I had just written the thing myself. So I gave myself one day to rebuild from scratch, with the modest goal of first-class support for \TeX , Python, Forester, and C/C++. That one-day sprint is long past, and the config has kept evolving since — this note describes where it landed, not the frantic first pass. The through-line is that almost everything now leans on Neovim's own primitives instead of a framework, which is exactly the transparency I was missing.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The whole config lives in my dotfiles. It is a thin init.lua that loads four modules — config.lazy, config.options, config.keymaps, and core.lsp — with the actual plugin specs under lua/plugins/ and per-server LSP tables under lsp/.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To my surprise, a lot of the behavior I had taken for granted turned out to be options LazyVim set internally. I was greeted on first launch by 8-space indents and a clipboard that would not survive a trip between terminal instances. So the first job was archaeology: I dug through LazyVim for every option I actually liked and copied them into config/options.lua. System clipboard, sensible folding via Treesitter (foldexpr = v:lua.vim.treesitter.foldexpr()), a global statusline, conceallevel for markup — the unglamorous foundation everything else sits on.

The plugin manager is lazy.nvim, the de facto standard, and it genuinely just works. The bootstrap snippet from their docs clones it on first run, and after that every file under lua/plugins/ that returns a spec table is imported automatically. No central manifest to keep in sync — a new file is a new plugin.

This is where the config diverged most sharply from where it started. The first pass used mason.nvim, mason-lspconfig, and nvim-lspconfig — the standard stack. All three are now gone. Neovim 0.11 ships a native LSP configuration API: vim.lsp.config defines a server and vim.lsp.enable turns it on, and any table returned from lsp/<name>.lua on the runtime path is picked up as that server's config. So core.lsp is just a list —

— and each server gets a plain Lua file in lsp/. No package manager, no bridge plugin translating between two config schemas. I install the servers themselves through my system package manager and let Neovim wire them up. It is dramatically less machinery for the same result, and when something breaks there is exactly one place to look.

The Python setup is the clearest upgrade. I dropped pyright for basedpyright and split responsibilities: basedpyright does type checking and inlay hints, while ruff runs as its own language server handling import organization and diagnostics. In the LazyVim days I fought constantly with import resolution, hand-writing a function to locate each project's virtualenv and pin pythonPath to its interpreter. basedpyright resolves environments well enough on its own that the hack is gone. clangd, texlab, and lua_ls round out the set, each configured in its own file — texlab in particular carries a fair amount of custom command wiring for building, forward search into Zathura, and environment manipulation.

A shared LspAttach autocommand in core.lsp binds the buffer-local LSP keymaps once, for every server: go-to-definition and friends route through fzf-lua pickers, and document highlighting, inlay-hint toggling, and native completion get enabled per-buffer based on what each client actually supports.

fr:title.text: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Forester finally has a real language server

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The original post described an ugly hack to get Forester completion. The forester.nvim plugin exposed a completion source, but only for nvim-cmp; blink.cmp (which I use — see below) needs a blink.compat shim, and that shim expected a plugin to be a source rather than to bury one inside a larger feature set. Getting reference completion working meant reading blink.compat internals and registering the source by hand.

None of that survives, because Forester now ships an actual language server. My lsp/forester.lua just launches forester lsp and hands it the forest root. The one wrinkle worth recording: forester locates the forest by its process working directory and ignores the LSP rootUri, and Neovim does not spawn the server in root_dir by default. So the config resolves the nearest forest.toml and starts the RPC process with an explicit cwd — otherwise the server exits with status 1 whenever Neovim's cwd has no forest.toml (e.g. when I open a tree from $HOME). Reference completion — indispensable for writing this very blog — now flows through the standard LSP path with no shim in sight.

Completion is blink.cmp, the successor that finally displaced nvim-cmp. Its sources are lsp, path, snippets, and omni, with LuaSnip as the snippet engine. Because I lean on native LSP, the on_attach handler calls vim.lsp.completion.enable with autotrigger, so the server's completions feed the picker directly.

Layered on top is supermaven-nvim for AI ghost-text suggestions — accept-suggestion, accept-word, and clear bound to <C-l>, <C-j>, and <C-]>. It fills the role Copilot-style tools play elsewhere without getting in the way of the structured LSP and snippet completions.

Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always wanted snippets that auto-expand. Under LazyVim I had settled for friendly-snippets on native Neovim snippets; rebuilding from scratch, I refused to compromise. The engine is LuaSnip, which blink.cmp supports natively, and I have to plug the luasnip-latex-snippets repo that ports the original UltiSnips math snippets over. My own snippets live in a small local plugin, custom-luasnip-snippets.nvim, vendored under lua/local/.

The trick I had wanted for years and can finally do: load LaTeX math snippets while inside math environments in Forester. The snippet plugin runs with use_treesitter = true, so it inspects the Forester Treesitter grammar, finds the node types for math environments, and only expands the math snippets when the cursor is actually inside one. Writing \TeX inside a .tree file feels exactly like writing it in a .tex file.

Formatting runs through conform.nvim on save. Python is handled by ruff_format followed by pyink, Lua by stylua — this replaced the old black + isort pairing, folding most of it into the same ruff that already runs as a language server. Formatting falls back to the LSP formatter when no dedicated tool is configured for a filetype.

The fuzzy finder is fzf-lua, which replaced Telescope. File and grep pickers are scoped to a detected project root — a small utils.root module (lifted from LazyVim) walks LSP workspace folders, .git, and cwd to find it — with a dedicated tex override that restricts live-grep to \TeX source files.

The piece I am most fond of is forester-new, a local plugin that wires the editor into the forest's own note pipeline. It shells out to scripts/new so every tree gets the standard frontmatter and routing, then commands like :ForesterNew, :ForesterStub, :ForesterCitek, and :ForesterTransient handle the follow-through: creating a tree and inserting a \transclude, stubbing a concept and dropping a [[id]] link at the cursor, or — in visual mode — lifting a selection into a new tree and replacing it in place with the transclude. Stub-and-link without ever leaving the sentence, which is exactly the friction the forest workflow is supposed to remove.

I originally built a whole in-editor Anki workflow on top of AnkiConnect and a vendored anki.nvim. That has since moved out of the editor and into the forest itself: cards are just \taxon{Card} trees, and scripts/anki-sync mirrors them into Anki — creating the note on first sync and updating in place thereafter. Writing a card is now the same as writing any other tree, snippets and all, and the sync is one command away. This is an example of a card.
The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.

fr:title.text: Idea Purgatory: Skills for Offline Goal-Conditioned RL

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Idea Purgatory: Skills for Offline Goal-Conditioned RL

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This idea is a combination of a few relevant papers in offline skill learning. Namely, the problem of how to learn re-usable, temporally-extended objects purely from offline data.

fr:title.text: POTW: Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: POTW: Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The setup for Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options:

html:ul.html:li: We have a meta-controller that outputs the skill z given the state s i.e. z_t \sim \eta (\cdot \mid s_t)
We have the skill, which is a skill-conditioned policy a_t \sim \pi (\cdot \mid s_t, z_t)

We have the termination function \psi (\cdot \mid s_t, z_{t - 1}), which is per skill and evaluates the previous skill z_{t - 1} at the new state s_t, such that b_t \sim \mathrm {Ber}\big (\psi (s_t, z_{t - 1})\big ) \in \{0, 1\}
For b_t = 1, we resample a new skill z_t \sim \eta (\cdot \mid s_t)
Otherwise, z_{t} = z_{t - 1}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Assuming there is a true underlying latent history \xi = \{b_{0}, z_{0}, b_{1}, z_{1}, \ldots \} and the observable history \mathcal {H} = \{s_{0}, a_{0}, s_{1}, a_{1}, \ldots \} , the likelihood according to the above generative model is given by
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(\mathcal {H}, \xi ) = p(s_{0})\delta _{\{b_{0} = 1\}}\eta (z_{0} \mid s_{0}) \prod _{t=1}^{T - 1} \mathbb {P}(b_t, z_t \mid z_{t - 1}, s_t) \prod _{t = 0}^{T - 1} \pi (a_t \mid s_t, z_t) p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Furthermore, according to our model, we have that

Up to this point, we have assumed this is the ground truth model and we are given each of the components. Instead we want to learn these components. To do so, we introduce our set of parameters \mathbf {\theta } , such that each component is parametrized by a subset of \mathbf {\theta } i.e. \eta _{\theta }, \pi _{\theta }, \psi _{\theta }.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn some \theta that maximized the likelihood of the observable data.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: To do so, first consider the log-likelihood of our new parametrized model
Since we are only concerned with learning \theta , we can disregard the \theta independent terms leaving
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. This requires samples (\mathcal {H}, \xi ) \sim P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}, \xi ), but we only have samples \mathcal {H} \sim P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H})!!!

Solution. We can use the trick from Expectation Maximization. Referred to in the paper as the expectation gradient trick. Namely, \nabla _{\theta } \log P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}) = \mathbb {E}_{\xi \sim P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H})} \left [ \nabla _{\theta } \log P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}, \xi ) \right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now it remains to compute the expectation from P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H})
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H}) is a distribution over (K, \{0,1\})^{T} i.e. over each (z_t, b_t). To efficiently compute, we need to use Forward-backward algorithm.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For,
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} u_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(z_t = z | \xi ) \\ v_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(b_t = 1, z_t = z | \xi ) \\ w_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(z_t = z, b_{t+1} = 0 | \xi ). \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: We have that
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex:
abla _{	heta } L[	heta ; \xi ] = \sum _{h \in \mathcal {H}} \left ( \sum _{t=0}^{T-1} \left ( v_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log \eta (z|s_t) + u_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log \pi _h(a_t|s_t) ight ) + \sum _{t=0}^{T-2} \left ( (u_t(z) - w_t(z)) 
abla _{	heta } \log \psi _h(s_{t+1}) + w_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log (1 - \psi _h(s_{t+1})) ight ) ight ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Following the Forward-backward algorithm, we can get the posterior for the latent i.e. \mathbb {P}((z_{t}, b_{t}) \mid \mathcal {H}). We have the corresponding forward and backward components
which can then be written as
Forward pass. For the forward pass, we have the initial condition

Here (z', b') ranges over the possible values of (z_{t - 1}, b_{t - 1}). It is sufficient to expand the remaining two terms in terms of our generative model as defined above. First, note that (z_{t}, b_{t}) are independent of b_{t - 1}. Therefore, we can drop the b_{t - 1} term and are left with the following two cases:
Plugging this in, we now have
It remains to find \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(s_t, a_t \mid z, b). Once again, given z_t we no longer care about b_t. Therefore, this becomes
This gives us our final expression
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. We have abused notation here, allowing \mathbb {P}(s_t, a_t \mid z_t, b_t) to depend on s_{t - 1}, a_{t - 1}, etc. This is to align with the notation from Forward-backward algorithm. It makes no difference in practice as long as the backward dependence is finite / bounded. However, in practice you would want the history dependence to be small and here it is only one-step.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backward pass. For the backward pass, we define the end term as
Here (z', b') ranges over the possible values of (z_{t + 1}, b_{t + 1}). The other terms are analagous i.e.
and
The setup for Learning Options via Compression.

html:ul.html:li: A state abstraction posterior q_{\theta }(s_{t} \mid z_{t}, x_{t}) and decoder p_{\theta }(a_{t} \mid s_{t}), where \pi _{\theta }(a_t \mid s_t, x_t) = \mathbb {E}_{q_{\theta }(s_{t} \mid x_{t}, z_{t})}[p_{\theta }(a_t \mid s_t)]
This seems to be needed to ensure that the decoder cannot overfit to the offline data?
A termination network \beta _{\theta }(b_t \mid x_{1}, \ldots , x_{t})
A skill posterior \eta _{\theta }(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}, b_{t}, x_{1:T},a_{1:T}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in DDO, where they do exact posterior inference, here we do variational inference. Namely, they directly try to model the posterior via the neural network \eta _{\theta }(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}, b_{t}, x_{1:T},a_{1:T}). This has a few consequences:

html:ul.html:li: The expectation gradient is now true Expectation Maximization. In particular, we need to optimize the ELBO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The ELBO of LOVE can be written as
Notably this requires a few additional prior networks for the KL regularization term in the ELBO.

html:ul.html:li: A boundary prior \beta (b_t \mid s_{t}).
A skill prior p(z).
A state abstraction transition prior p(s_t | s_{t-1}, z_t).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Additionally, we also need to sample the z's. This is done via Gumbel softmax and causes much higher variance.

html:figure.html:figcaption: How LOVE goes from input sequence to end skill labels.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We find ourselves in-between LOVE and DDO. In particular, in DDO, they are concerned with learning a hierarchy of policies where the higher-level meta policy \eta must be usable online only given the current state.

html:ul.html:li: (+) The learned termination conditions \beta are dependent on the active skill.
(+) Exact inference \implies lower variance.
(-) Weak skill model \eta (z \mid s).
(-) No explicit encouragement for temporally-extended skills.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On the contrary, in LOVE, they treat skills as a pure sequence-modeling problem. The posterior q_{\theta }(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}, b_{t}, x_{0}, \ldots , x_{T}) is purely used to label offline trajectories and give the skills meaning. To make them usable online, it is then required to learn an additional hierarchical policy over these skills.

html:ul.html:li: (+) The skill-labeler is allowed to use not only the current state but also the future visited states.
(+) Compression objective encourages temporally extended skills.
(-) Design inherently does not allow the terminations to be skill-dependent.
(-) Need to sample z's to compute gradients \implies higher-variance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Our graphical model. We try to combine the desirable properties.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Each boundary depends on a skill and each skill depends on the whole sequence. Each boundary also depends on the whole history. However, the skill conditioned policy only requires the current state s and skill z. Thus, allowing the skills to be used.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We essentially combine the insight of LOVE where instead of trying to model the full joint distribution i.e. \mathbb {P}(\mathcal {H}, \xi ) we instead model \mathbb {P}(\mathcal {A}, \xi \mid \mathcal {S}), where we treat the entire state history as known.

In the language of HMMs, the emission probabilities only correspond to the actions. Therefore, we can perform the Forward-backward algorithm and condition on whatever component of the state we want without breaking the exactness.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This is only a good idea for us because we do not need to use the meta controller during evaluation.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Deliberation cost. In LOVE, they encourage temporal extension through the compression loss. This requires an empirical approximation of the skillmarginal p(z). For me, this caused the skill entropy to just collapse to zero instead of encouraging temporally extended options. Instead, we use a deliberation cost more common in the options literature The Option-Critic Architecture, where we just add the switch-rate as an additional loss term.

html:ul.html:li: We are given the posterior \mathbb {P}(z_t = z, b_t = 1 \mid \mathcal {H}). In our implementation this would be (B, T, K)
We marginalize over skills z by summing to get \mathbb {P}(b_t = 1 \mid \mathcal {H})
Then average over batch B and time T to get expected reads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: As in LOVE, we need to balance minimizing log-likelihood with compression / switching cost. To do so, we do dual descent.

html:ul.html:li: First, without compression, we run the algorithm and observe what is approximately the best attainable log-likelihood loss is.
Then, we minimize the deliberation cost subject to \mathcal {L}_{\text {likelihood}} \leq C + \epsilon
The skill network \eta (z_t \mid s_{0}, \ldots s_{T}) is a bidirectional GRU, where the hidden states are accumulated via running the GRU forwards and backwards and concatenating the results.
The termination network \beta (b_t \mid z_{t - 1}, s_{0}, \ldots , s_{t}) is a GRU, that only runs forward.
Instead of passing the previous skill as input, the output dimension is size K (number of skills).
The skill policy network \pi _{\theta }(a \mid s, z) is a standard Gaussian policy that take z as a one-hot encoding.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Setup. Assume we are given a set of skills \mathbf {z} \in \{0, \ldots , K\} along with a skill-conditioned lower-level policy \pi (s \mid z). Then what we want is to be able to learn the optimal goal-reaching policy over these skills. To do so, we must go beyond traditional offline skill-learning algorithms. We must Learn a value function over learned skills purely from offline data

Extending beyond pointmaze in locomotion. The main issue faced here is that our discriminator is too powerful in high dimensional state space. Namely, we score options based on their ability to reconstruct actions i.e. if \mathbb {P}(a_t \mid s_t, z_t = z) is high then we further increase the probability of selecting that option z. In higher-dimensional state spaces, the skill network \pi _{\theta }(a_t \cdot \mid s_t, z) is powerful enough to reconstruct a_t purely from s_t without using z. This basically provides no signal for which z to use, and we end up just selecting z uniformly every time step.

html:ul.html:li: I have seen a little sign of life from trying \pi (a \mid f_{\theta }(s), z) i.e. compressing s to a lower dimensional representation.
It may also be worth trying a really small network. Right now (256, 256) already gets very low reconstruction loss, so maybe we can go smaller?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Conquering manipulation. In OGBench: Benchmarking Offline Goal-Conditioned RL, they provide a suite of manipulation environments in addition to the standard locomotion. One such environment, is the manipulation equivalent of the lights out game. This is a great test-bed to separate skill-learning from planning. In particular, the skill-learning portion can be clearly decomposed into the pressing of each of the buttons. However, when directly trying to use the LOVE it is not able to distinguish between these skills.

html:ul.html:li: On puzzle 3x3 LOVE terminates every 1.4 steps because all the skills look similar.
This is using the threshold \beta > 0.9.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pressing each of the buttons looks too similar, where the main discrepancy between them is only their termination condition. Since LOVE's termination condition is independent of skill, it is unable to distinguish between these.

html:ul.html:li: This is further confirmed in the scene environment. LOVE is able to distinguish between very different skills like open drawer vs. window. However, it is not able to distinguish between unlock drawer vs. window, where they only differ in pressing two adjacent buttons.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Injecting goals into the graphical model. Another failure mode of manipulation is that skills cannot be goal-independent. For example, in the generating policy of scene they have a pick and place skill that picks up the block and places it somewhere. Where to place the block is encoded in the goal. We can augment our graphical model to also allow the actions to depend on the goal.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Our graphical model with the goal included.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, we do not want the skills to become goal-conditioned policies. To get around this we can use the information bottleneck idea from InfoBot: Transfer and Exploration via the Information Bottleneck. Namely, we penalize the Mutual Information between the goal and action or goal and skill (TBD the right way to do this).
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Ideally, this would allow goal-dependent skills, where the goal is not the entire state space. For example, penalizing Mutual Information could cause just the desired block position as the information to be passed to the skill. This ignores the irrelavant components of the state space for that skill. Another skill may focus on whether the window is locked / unlocked, etc.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Get rid of weird threshold / turn into MPC-ish. Currently, when planning, we must tune the value_threshold for when we pass control to goal-conditioned policy. To get 89 percent on pointmaze we used -10. Instead of using a threshold, we could instead just treat the goal-conditioned policy as another skill, but the future state is just .
The setup for Learning Options via Compression.

html:ul.html:li: A state abstraction posterior q_{\theta }(s_{t} \mid z_{t}, x_{t}) and decoder p_{\theta }(a_{t} \mid s_{t}), where \pi _{\theta }(a_t \mid s_t, x_t) = \mathbb {E}_{q_{\theta }(s_{t} \mid x_{t}, z_{t})}[p_{\theta }(a_t \mid s_t)]
This seems to be needed to ensure that the decoder cannot overfit to the offline data?
A termination network \beta _{\theta }(b_t \mid x_{1}, \ldots , x_{t})
A skill posterior \eta _{\theta }(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}, b_{t}, x_{1:T},a_{1:T}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Unlike in DDO, where they do exact posterior inference, here we do variational inference. Namely, they directly try to model the posterior via the neural network \eta _{\theta }(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}, b_{t}, x_{1:T},a_{1:T}). This has a few consequences:

html:ul.html:li: The expectation gradient is now true Expectation Maximization. In particular, we need to optimize the ELBO.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The ELBO of LOVE can be written as
Notably this requires a few additional prior networks for the KL regularization term in the ELBO.

html:ul.html:li: A boundary prior \beta (b_t \mid s_{t}).
A skill prior p(z).
A state abstraction transition prior p(s_t | s_{t-1}, z_t).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Additionally, we also need to sample the z's. This is done via Gumbel softmax and causes much higher variance.

html:figure.html:figcaption: How LOVE goes from input sequence to end skill labels.

fr:title.text: POTW: Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: POTW: Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The setup for Multi-Level Discovery of Deep Options:

html:ul.html:li: We have a meta-controller that outputs the skill z given the state s i.e. z_t \sim \eta (\cdot \mid s_t)
We have the skill, which is a skill-conditioned policy a_t \sim \pi (\cdot \mid s_t, z_t)

We have the termination function \psi (\cdot \mid s_t, z_{t - 1}), which is per skill and evaluates the previous skill z_{t - 1} at the new state s_t, such that b_t \sim \mathrm {Ber}\big (\psi (s_t, z_{t - 1})\big ) \in \{0, 1\}
For b_t = 1, we resample a new skill z_t \sim \eta (\cdot \mid s_t)
Otherwise, z_{t} = z_{t - 1}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Assuming there is a true underlying latent history \xi = \{b_{0}, z_{0}, b_{1}, z_{1}, \ldots \} and the observable history \mathcal {H} = \{s_{0}, a_{0}, s_{1}, a_{1}, \ldots \} , the likelihood according to the above generative model is given by
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(\mathcal {H}, \xi ) = p(s_{0})\delta _{\{b_{0} = 1\}}\eta (z_{0} \mid s_{0}) \prod _{t=1}^{T - 1} \mathbb {P}(b_t, z_t \mid z_{t - 1}, s_t) \prod _{t = 0}^{T - 1} \pi (a_t \mid s_t, z_t) p(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Furthermore, according to our model, we have that

Up to this point, we have assumed this is the ground truth model and we are given each of the components. Instead we want to learn these components. To do so, we introduce our set of parameters \mathbf {\theta } , such that each component is parametrized by a subset of \mathbf {\theta } i.e. \eta _{\theta }, \pi _{\theta }, \psi _{\theta }.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn some \theta that maximized the likelihood of the observable data.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: To do so, first consider the log-likelihood of our new parametrized model
Since we are only concerned with learning \theta , we can disregard the \theta independent terms leaving
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. This requires samples (\mathcal {H}, \xi ) \sim P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}, \xi ), but we only have samples \mathcal {H} \sim P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H})!!!

Solution. We can use the trick from Expectation Maximization. Referred to in the paper as the expectation gradient trick. Namely, \nabla _{\theta } \log P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}) = \mathbb {E}_{\xi \sim P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H})} \left [ \nabla _{\theta } \log P_{\theta }(\mathcal {H}, \xi ) \right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now it remains to compute the expectation from P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H})
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that P_{\theta }(\xi \mid \mathcal {H}) is a distribution over (K, \{0,1\})^{T} i.e. over each (z_t, b_t). To efficiently compute, we need to use Forward-backward algorithm.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For,
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} u_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(z_t = z | \xi ) \\ v_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(b_t = 1, z_t = z | \xi ) \\ w_t(z) &= \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(z_t = z, b_{t+1} = 0 | \xi ). \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: We have that
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex:
abla _{	heta } L[	heta ; \xi ] = \sum _{h \in \mathcal {H}} \left ( \sum _{t=0}^{T-1} \left ( v_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log \eta (z|s_t) + u_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log \pi _h(a_t|s_t) ight ) + \sum _{t=0}^{T-2} \left ( (u_t(z) - w_t(z)) 
abla _{	heta } \log \psi _h(s_{t+1}) + w_t(z) 
abla _{	heta } \log (1 - \psi _h(s_{t+1})) ight ) ight ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Following the Forward-backward algorithm, we can get the posterior for the latent i.e. \mathbb {P}((z_{t}, b_{t}) \mid \mathcal {H}). We have the corresponding forward and backward components
which can then be written as
Forward pass. For the forward pass, we have the initial condition

Here (z', b') ranges over the possible values of (z_{t - 1}, b_{t - 1}). It is sufficient to expand the remaining two terms in terms of our generative model as defined above. First, note that (z_{t}, b_{t}) are independent of b_{t - 1}. Therefore, we can drop the b_{t - 1} term and are left with the following two cases:
Plugging this in, we now have
It remains to find \mathbb {P}_{\theta }(s_t, a_t \mid z, b). Once again, given z_t we no longer care about b_t. Therefore, this becomes
This gives us our final expression
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. We have abused notation here, allowing \mathbb {P}(s_t, a_t \mid z_t, b_t) to depend on s_{t - 1}, a_{t - 1}, etc. This is to align with the notation from Forward-backward algorithm. It makes no difference in practice as long as the backward dependence is finite / bounded. However, in practice you would want the history dependence to be small and here it is only one-step.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Backward pass. For the backward pass, we define the end term as
Here (z', b') ranges over the possible values of (z_{t + 1}, b_{t + 1}). The other terms are analagous i.e.
and
Suppose we have a sequence of latent variable \xi = (z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots , z_{T}) and the corresponding sequence of observations \mathcal {H} = (x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots , x_{T})
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to compute the posterior distribution \mathbb {P}(\xi \mid \mathcal {H}).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Suppose \mathcal {z} is a discrete random variable taking at most K different values. Then, for horizon length T, there are K^T possible values for \xi . Thus, computing \mathbb {P}(\xi \mid \mathcal {H}) blows up exponentially.

html:div.html:p: Suppose that z \in \{0, 1\} and T = 2. Then

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Directly computing the probability for each possible value of \xi would thus take O(K^T) or exponential in the horizon length. Can we do better?
In the particular case of a hidden markov model, we can achieve O(TK^2)!!!

A hidden markov model is a statistical model, where the hidden states h \in \mathcal {H} form a Markov Chain, such that \mathbb {P}(h_{t+1} \mid h_{1}, \ldots , h_{t}) = \mathbb {P}(h_{t + 1} \mid h_{t})
Unlike a Markov chain, in addition to the hidden states, a hidden markov models contains observations x \in \mathcal {X} with associated emission probabilities \mathbb {P}(x_t \mid h_t).

html:figure.html:figcaption: Graphical model of a hidden markov model.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: From the graphical model, we can see that for any i < j, (x_{0}, \ldots , x_i) \perp (x_{j}, \ldots , x_{N}) \mid z_{i}. Or in english, observations are independent conditioned on the hidden state.
In our setup, we can rewrite the marginal as
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid \mathcal {H}) = \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid x_{0}, \ldots , x_{T})
fr:mainmatter.html:p: From Baye's rule, we can decompose this as
Since we are considering an HMM, (x_{1}, \ldots , x_t) and (x_{t + 1}, \ldots , x_{T}) are conditionally independent conditioned on z_t. Thus, we can further simplify this to
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid x_{0}, \ldots , x_{T}) = \frac {\mathbb {P}(x_{t + 1}, \ldots , x_{T} \mid z_t) \mathbb {P}(x_{0}, \ldots , x_{t}, z_t)}{\mathbb {P}(x_{0}, \ldots , x_{T})}
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. We will now consider the terms in the numerator separately. The first can be viewed as the backward term \beta _t(z) = \mathbb {P}(x_{t + 1}, \ldots , x_{T} \mid z_t = z), and the second as the forward term \phi _t(z) = \mathbb {P}(x_{0}, \ldots , x_{t}, z_t = z). Naming convention is to denote which direction in time information propagates (we will see shortly).
Remark. Both passes are O(TK^2) because at each timestep, it requires computing \mathbb {P}(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}) or \mathbb {P}(z_{t + 1} \mid z_{t}) which is a K \times K transition matrix.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Notably, the structure of the HMM ensures each marginal \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid \mathcal {H}) depends only on the local transition probabilities \mathbb {P}(z_{t} \mid z_{t - 1}) and \mathbb {P}(z_{t + 1} \mid z_{t}) rather than the exponential global \mathbb {P}(z_{0}, \ldots , z_{T}). This lends itself to the idea of a dynamic programming algorithm, where we compute the posterior of the preceding / succeeding latent and use the result for the next. Since we have bi-directional dependencies this requires making two passes a forward and backward pass. The key remaining detail is that these components can be efficiently combined to recover the full true marginal.
Finally, all that remains is to combine \phi and \beta to recover the true posterior. Recall that these directly correspond to the components in the numerator i.e.
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid \mathcal {H}) = \frac {\phi _t(z) \beta _t(z)}{\mathbb {P}(\mathcal {H})}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Therefore, it remains to compute the normalization term \mathbb {P}(\mathcal {H}). To do so, note that
Thus, the true posterior can be recovered as
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \mathbb {P}(z_t \mid \mathcal {H}) = \frac {\phi _t(z) \beta _t(z)}{\sum _{z} \phi _t(z)\beta _t(z)}
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since z is discrete, this sum is only O(K) and therefore easily computable.
Given a topology and a packet's source and destination, which path through the network does it take? Three classes of answer:

html:ul.html:li:
Greedy (shortest-path) — always pick the shortest available route.
(+) Minimizes latency under light load.
(-) Overloads central links; worst-case throughput poor.

Uniform / oblivious random — pick a direction at random.
(+) Spreads load; avoids systematic hotspots.
(-) Wastes bandwidth on non-minimal paths; latency unpredictable.

Adaptive — at each hop, pick the outgoing link with the lowest local congestion.
(+) Dynamically balances load; best worst-case throughput.
(-) Routers need congestion state; risk of livelock without care.

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Example. Tornado traffic on an 8-node bidirectional ring: node i sends to (i+3) \bmod 8.
Greedy: all packets travel counter-clockwise. Bidirectional bandwidth wasted; \frac {1}{3} utilization.
Uniform: randomly clockwise/counter-clockwise. Counter-clockwise gets \frac {2}{3} utilization, clockwise \frac {2}{5}; worst-case \frac {2}{5}.
Adaptive: sends \frac {5}{8} counter-clockwise to match traffic exactly. Best.
Remark. Adaptive routing tends to need Network backpressure signaling to know where congestion is, and care to avoid livelock when many adaptive decisions chase each other.

Remark. Oblivious strategies like Dimension-order routing and Valiant's routing algorithm give deterministic guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded path length) at the cost of giving up the load-balancing of true adaptive routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Mesh topology with wrap-around links connecting the ends of each dimension. In the k-ary n-cube classification, a k-ary n-cube. Uniform node degree (every node has degree 2n), uniform load distribution, half the hop count of a mesh.

html:ul.html:li: Every node has degree 2n.
Average hop count for even k: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{4}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/8 — half that of a Mesh topology.
Good path diversity: many shortest paths between any two nodes.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Wrap-around links are physically long. To equalize wire delays, the torus must be folded, doubling effective wire lengths and link area. The wrap-around also doubles the link count versus a mesh of the same dimensions.
Remark. Despite the theoretical advantage, mesh wins on real chips. Tori show up in HPC interconnects (Cray) where the wires can run on dedicated cables.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Variants:

html:ul.html:li: k-ary n-cube: k nodes along each of n dimensions, all ends wrapped.
Asymmetric: (i, j, k)-ary — different node count per dimension.
Mesh: same as torus but without wrap-around.
Ring: k-ary 1-cube.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A 2D grid of nodes with neighbors connected on the four compass directions, no wrap-around. The dominant on-chip topology in commercial designs (Xeon Phi, recent server x86 chips) because it lays out naturally on a 2D die.

html:ul.html:li: Interior nodes degree 4; edge nodes degree 3; corners degree 2.
Average hop count for an even-k mesh: H_{avg} = \frac {nk}{3}
Maximum channel load for uniform random traffic: k/4 — twice that of a Torus topology.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Non-uniform node degree. Edge nodes carry less traffic than they could, while interior nodes bottleneck on traffic flowing through them.

Remark. Mesh dominates Torus topology in practice despite the topology metrics being worse, because torus wrap-around wires are physically long and force expensive folding to equalize link lengths. Manufacturing wins over theoretical performance.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A direct network where each node connects to two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Bidirectional rings allow either direction of travel; unidirectional rings allow only one. The simplest topology to implement and reason about.
In the k-ary n-cube family, a ring is a k-ary 1-cube.

html:ul.html:li: Degree 2 (every node).
Diameter \lfloor k/2 \rfloor for a bidirectional k-node ring.
Bisection bandwidth 2 — cut the ring at exactly two links.
Hardware overhead very low: routers have two ports.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Worst-case hop count is O(k). Doesn't scale beyond a small number of nodes.
Remark. Despite the poor scaling, rings are common in commercial CPUs (Intel's L3 ring bus on Sandy/Ivy Bridge) because they're cheap and 8–16 cores is enough.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies that achieves provably optimal worst-case throughput by introducing randomness in path selection:

html:ol.html:li: For a packet going from s to d, randomly pick an intermediate node w.
Route s \to w using Dimension-order routing.
Eject the packet at w, then re-inject it.
Route w \to d using Dimension-order routing.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To preserve deadlock-freedom, the intermediate node must remove the packet from the network before re-injecting — otherwise the channel dependencies of the two halves could interlock.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Restricting w to lie in the sub-quadrant defined by s and d keeps paths near-minimal while still spreading load — the practical compromise between guaranteed throughput and added latency.

Remark. Valiant's algorithm trades average-case latency (paths are longer) for worst-case throughput (any traffic pattern looks roughly uniform once randomized). The right call for adversarial workloads; overkill for uniform traffic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: An oblivious Network routing strategies for k-ary n-cube networks (Mesh topology, Torus topology): always exhaust the offset in one dimension completely before changing to the next. E.g. in 2D mesh routing, route X-then-Y — never turn back to X after starting Y.

The dimension order prevents routing deadlock: cycles in the channel dependency graph are impossible because no packet ever requests a channel in a "lower" dimension once it has started using a higher one. The dependency graph is therefore acyclic, and the standard cycle-equals-deadlock argument applies.

html:ul.html:li: (+) Provably deadlock-free without virtual channels.
(-) Zero path diversity. Hot spots can't be routed around.
(-) Unaware of current network state — oblivious.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The standard remedy for the path-diversity problem is Valiant's routing algorithm, which keeps the deadlock-freedom of dimension-order routing while spreading load with a randomized intermediate hop.

A multistage indirect network. A k-ary n-fly has k^n terminal nodes connected through n stages of k \times k crossbar switches. At each stage, the routing decision selects which of k sub-groups to enter; after n stages, every terminal can reach every other.

html:ul.html:li: Hop count n - 1.
Under uniform random traffic, maximum channel load is 1 (perfectly balanced).
Channel load drops further for non-uniform traffic that respects the natural partitioning.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. Zero path diversity. There is exactly one path between any source and destination. A single hot link wedges traffic.
Solution. Add shuffle stages that permute paths after each routing stage, so the same source-destination pair can be reached by multiple routes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A tree topology with bandwidth that doubles at every level toward the root. Each interior switch has logical degree 4, but as you climb the tree, link width doubles to absorb the combined traffic of its subtree — preserving bisection bandwidth.

Structurally, a fat tree is a folded Clos network: the input and output stages of a Clos are merged into a single bidirectional stage at each level of the tree, and the middle stage becomes the higher levels of the tree.

html:ul.html:li: Each switch has logical degree 4 (2 down, 2 up — with link doubling).
Bisection bandwidth scales linearly with the number of leaves.
Path diversity grows toward the root.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Fat trees are the dominant topology for off-chip HPC interconnects (InfiniBand fat-tree clusters). Less common on-chip because the doubling links are hard to lay out on a 2D die.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 4mN = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

html:li.fr:link: Deepseek v1 through R1: RL is back!
